points.text: Gather baseline metrics: cache hit/miss rates, DB query counts, CPU usage, latency before and after the change.
Identify new query parameters and examine how they affect cache key generation; ensure keys remain consistent.
Check for cache key explosion: many unique keys reduce hit rate; consider normalizing or hashing parameters.
Review cache eviction policies and TTL; verify that added parameters aren't causing premature expiration.
Profile the application to locate CPU hotspots; determine if extra parameter handling or serialization is costly.
Analyze DB query plans for the new parameter combinations; add indexes or rewrite queries to reduce reads.
Run a controlled A/B test disabling the new parameters to confirm impact on cache and DB load.
Implement cache warm‑up or pre‑populate common key patterns to improve hit rate after deployment.
Collect baseline metrics: cache hit rate, DB reads, CPU, and query latency before and after the feature rollout.
Log all SQL statements executed by the new feature, including parameter values, to identify new or changed queries.
Compare execution plans of affected queries to detect scans, missing indexes, or parameter sniffing issues.
Check that statistics are up‑to‑date; run UPDATE STATISTICS if plan choices have degraded.
Add or adjust indexes on columns introduced as query parameters to support selective filtering.
If parameter sniffing causes suboptimal plans, use OPTION (RECOMPILE) or OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN for those queries.
Monitor cache eviction and memory pressure; ensure cache size is sufficient for increased workload.
Profile CPU usage per query to pinpoint hot queries that are driving the CPU increase.
Consider query result caching or materialized views for frequently accessed, read‑only result sets.
Iteratively test each change in a staging environment, measuring cache hit rate and DB read reductions.
Collect time‑series metrics for cache hit/miss, DB query count, CPU, latency from monitoring (e.g., Prometheus).
Correlate the deployment timestamp of the new feature with the metric shift to confirm causality.
Inspect the new query parameters: determine if they alter cache keys or bypass the cache layer.
Review cache key generation logic; ensure optional parameters are normalized or excluded to maintain hits.
Check cache eviction settings (size, TTL) for increased churn caused by higher request diversity.
Profile the application (e.g., perf, eBPF) to locate CPU hotspots introduced by parsing or processing new params.
Analyze DB query plans for the new requests; add indexes or rewrite queries to reduce per‑request cost.
Run a controlled load test with and without the new parameters to quantify impact and validate mitigations.
Iteratively apply mitigations (key normalization, cache size increase, index addition) and monitor hit rate and DB load.
